Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. At 29, I've got a lot going on outside of school, and I just kind of fit studying in wherever I could. Most weeks I'd tell myself I'd get organized, but then I'd end up cramming or just watching the lecture videos late at night when I finally had some quiet time. It was pretty scattered, and I know that's not ideal, but that's just how it went for me.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly based on deadlines or how I was feeling that week. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until an assignment was due. I tried to be consistent a few times, but life just kept getting in the way, and honestly, I didn't have the motivation to stick to a schedule. Deadlines were really the only thing that got me moving.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting an email notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, but I wouldn't always do much once I was there. If I'm being real, I didn't feel pulled to the platform—it was more like obligation than genuine interest. I'd scroll through, maybe watch part of a video, then log out.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became something I naturally went to. I tried the gamified tasks they introduced, and those were kind of interesting for a bit—earning points or badges felt like a small win. But even that novelty wore off pretty quickly. It never stuck as a habit; it always felt like something I had to force myself to do.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and actually see what was being explained, which helped more than reading dense text. I watched a decent amount of video content, even if I didn't always engage with the other stuff. The discussion boards were okay sometimes when other students explained things in simpler terms.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and a lot of the discussion posts. The readings felt long and dry, and I just couldn't get myself to focus on them. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The peer interaction stuff also felt forced—I'd do the minimum when required, but I didn't see the point in going beyond that.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate payoff, like it would help me pass an exam or complete an assignment. If something felt abstract or disconnected from my grades, I'd skip it. The gamified tasks were a bit different because they gave instant feedback and felt less like a chore, at least initially. But if something took too long with no clear benefit, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, I just didn't have the energy or motivation to do it. Required stuff I'd at least attempt, even if I did it last minute and half-heartedly. There's only so much bandwidth I have, and optional work just didn't make the cut when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and videos early on so I could look at them offline. I'd study from those on my tablet without being connected to the LMS at all. Sometimes I'd also use YouTube videos or other websites to understand concepts better because the course materials alone weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing downloaded slides or rewatching a video I'd saved, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also sometimes just sit and think through problems in my head or sketch things out on paper. The LMS only sees clicks and logins—it doesn't see me staring at my notes at 2 AM trying to make sense of things.
Q11: Yeah, I did that a lot actually. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then never open it on the LMS again. It was easier to have everything saved locally so I didn't have to deal with logging in or slow internet. So my engagement looked way lower than it actually was, at least in terms of time spent with the materials.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I was dealing with some personal stuff that made me feel pretty low—sad, I guess, if I'm honest. When I'm in that headspace, even simple tasks feel overwhelming. I'd look at the LMS and just feel exhausted before I even started. Those weeks, I barely showed up at all.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was a big one—I'd come home drained and the last thing I wanted was to log into the LMS and do coursework. Competing responsibilities also ate into my time; I'm not a traditional student with nothing else going on. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes just avoid it entirely rather than ask for help. It was easier to ignore it than face how behind I was.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It's like my body was there but my brain had checked out. Those sessions were basically useless, even though they probably showed up as activity.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important, but I'd put it off because I didn't feel ready or motivated enough to tackle it. The longer I waited, the more anxious I got, which made me avoid it even more. It's a vicious cycle. Sometimes I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just avoiding the discomfort of starting.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. Without them, I'd drift endlessly. When a deadline was close, I'd suddenly become very active—logging in, scrambling to watch videos, trying to piece together an assignment. It wasn't healthy or effective, but that pressure was the only real motivator I had. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me, honestly. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally to the next thing or was too discouraged to revisit old work. If the feedback was positive, it felt nice for a moment, but it didn't make me more engaged going forward. Negative feedback just made me feel worse about myself, which didn't exactly inspire me to try harder.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for someone like me who needs flexibility. Having six online courses at once meant I was always juggling, and this course didn't stand out as one I needed to prioritize. The weekly modules felt repetitive, and I lost track of where I was supposed to be. A clearer, more forgiving structure might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made some tasks more approachable—earning points felt less intimidating than just submitting work to be judged. But overall, the course design assumed a level of consistency I couldn't maintain. Long readings without visual aids were hard for me. If there had been more interactive or visual content throughout, I might have engaged more naturally.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to figure things out but can't translate that into finished work. Maybe they're confused, overwhelmed, or just don't know how to start the assignments. You can click around a lot, watch videos, read discussions, but if you don't understand how to apply it or you're too stressed, nothing gets submitted. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: Yeah, that kind of describes me in a way. I passed the course and did okay on exams even though my LMS activity was pretty low. A lot of my real studying happened offline with downloaded materials or external resources. The LMS just doesn't capture everything. Someone can look disengaged online but still be putting in work where it doesn't get tracked.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that time and what's happening offline. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They also can't capture your mental state, your motivation, or whether life outside school is falling apart. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present and invested. I'll be honest, I wasn't really engaged this semester. I did what I had to do to pass, but my heart wasn't in it. That's a hard thing to admit.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad and unmotivated I felt most of the time. It wouldn't show the hours I spent staring at downloaded slides trying to make myself care. It wouldn't capture the guilt I felt for not doing better or the relief when the gamified tasks gave me small wins. My emotional state and the effort it took just to do the bare minimum—none of that shows up in the data.
